content.text:
Discuss with me the approach to handle 'S', 's' branch in @src/liblex.zig 

- Take specification @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md as the source of truth.
- Take the tests in @src/liblex.zig as the source of truth.
- Take the existing production code for code pattern, technique, convention.
- In this discussion, we focus on the intents, not the actual handling, to make a clear understanding about how many case we could handle and their conditions.

My idea:

- Scan the pseudoword.
- Handle literal case first.
- Handle apply, override, cancel...
- Handle out of scope.
- Anything else unreachable.

Come up with the approach, then consult the Oracle for another perspective, but prevent the Oracle from producing any example code. You should not write any code.
I’ll inspect the Telex spec, the existing `S`/`s` production branch, and the relevant tests, then form an intent-level taxonomy and ask Oracle to critique it without code examples.

# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Literal input characters

- When any of the follow characters added to the word, change to literal input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal Vietnamese spelling. `Z` existing in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_
- Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
- This rule is lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.

### Diacritics

Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
Tones have a separate section.

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
  characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Stroke

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
  existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
  valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
- Keep tone untouched.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Cancel rules

- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
- The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
  - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
  - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
- The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
- Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
  remains until the new word.
  - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

- The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
  character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
- Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
  `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
  - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
  - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
- The new input character is appended literally.
- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.

### Tones

Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
These vowel could take any tone.

#### Level (ngang)

- The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
  corresponding variants.
- If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.

#### Rising (sắc)

- Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
  -> `Ớ`.

#### Falling (huyền)

- Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
  -> `Ờ`.

#### Dipping-rising (hỏi)

- Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
  `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.

#### Rising-glottalized (ngã)

- Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
  -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.

#### Falling-glottalized (nặng)

- Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
  -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.

#### Apply rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
  position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
- If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.

#### Cancel rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
  the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Reset rules

- Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
  change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
  any tone (level), append the new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Override rules

- Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
  from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
  the word. The override tone is always successful.

#### Scan pseudo-word

- Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
  the tone in the correct position.
- The existing characters must not be empty.
- Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
- If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
  character to scan, then stop.
- If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
  backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
- After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
  `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
  `<consonant><vowels>`.

#### Tone position rules

The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:

- Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
  tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
- Exceptions:
  - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
- If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
- If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
  - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
  - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
    prioritize the first to the last in the list.
  - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
    `U` count as part of the consonant.
  - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
  - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
    consonants, put on second vowel.
  - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
- Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
- Could not put tone for other cases.

input.command: grep -n "fn .*pseudo\|Pseudoword\|tone position\|rising tone\|falling tone\|append F\|word without placeable" src/liblex.zig

content.thinking:
**Figuring out API tests**

I think I need to focus on tests after 1888 for the API, but it seems those are not relevant. Also, I probably don’t need the liblex.h file since there's no code involved. I want to make sure I formulate this correctly and consult the oracle for proper guidance. Additionally, I should include file references but avoid asking for code examples. That's definitely a good plan!

content.text: I found that `S`/`s` is currently a placeholder while the tests already define the rising-tone apply surface; there are no production helpers yet for pseudoword scanning or tone placement. Next I’ll get Oracle’s critique on the intent taxonomy only, explicitly excluding code examples.

input.task: Critique an intent-level approach for handling the 'S'/'s' branch in src/liblex.zig for a Vietnamese Telex input method. Do not produce any example code, pseudocode, or API designs. Stay at the behavioral taxonomy / decision-tree level only.
input.context: Source of truth: specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md and tests in src/liblex.zig. Existing production style is a single State.add switch branch per trigger character, explicit condition ordering, mutation through Span.init* constructors only, and every State.add action must decide buffer_modification_index. Current S/s branch is empty. The relevant spec says tone triggers S/F/R/X/J scan the pseudoword, then apply/cancel/override depending on existing tone state; out-of-scope falls back to literal. Literal rule says F/J/W become literal from new character position when they are not consumed by diacritics/tones; S is not in that formal non-Vietnamese list. Tests currently cover rising apply cases for level-toned pseudowords and literal-switch behavior for F/J on no placeable tone, but not cancel/override for S/s yet. Proposed taxonomy: (1) gate literal mode/empty/overflow conditions as literal first, (2) scan pseudoword, (3) handle literal/out-of-scope/no-placeable cases, (4) handle apply, override, cancel, (5) anything else unreachable. Please assess ordering, missing categories, and whether S/s should differ from F/J/J literal-index behavior because S can be a real Vietnamese consonant.

run.result:
1) **TL;DR**

Your taxonomy is close, but it needs one crucial split: **“append literally” is not the same as “switch to literal mode.”** For **`S`/`s`**, ordinary fallback should usually be **literal append only**; unlike **`F`/`J`/`W`**, it should **not** set `literal_index` just because the trigger was not consumed. The one clear exception is **tone cancel**, where the spec explicitly says to append the trigger literally **and** switch to literal from that new position.

2) **Recommended approach (simple path, M 1–3h including tests)**

1. **Keep the shared early passthrough gate**, but keep it narrow:
   - already in literal mode
   - `literal_index` already active
   - empty buffer  
   These should all short-circuit before scan.
   - Minor critique: **“overflow” is not really its own behavioral category** here; in this codebase it is already represented by `literal_index` having been set earlier.

2. **After the early gate, scan the pseudoword first**, as you proposed.  
   That is the right pivot for tone handling.

3. **Split the pre-mutation fallback outcomes more explicitly** than “literal/out-of-scope/no-placeable”:
   - **no vowels in pseudoword**
   - **has vowels, but no legal tone position exists**
   - optionally treat exact `QU`-style non-placeable cases as part of that second bucket  
   These are normal, user-visible fallbacks, not exceptional cases.

4. **Then classify by existing tone state, not by verb order.**
   The clean partition is:
   - **matching rising tone already present** → **cancel**
   - **different non-level tone present** → **override**
   - **all scanned vowels are level** → **apply**

   That is clearer than “apply, override, cancel” as a priority list, because those are not really competing actions; they are mutually exclusive states of the scanned pseudoword.

5. **For `S` specifically, keep literal-mode switching only on cancel.**
   - **Empty / no vowel / no placeable tone**: append `S`/`s` literally, **do not** enter literal mode.
   - **Cancel**: remove the rising tone, append `S`/`s` literally, **do** enter literal mode from the new character position.
   - **Apply / override**: mutate in place only; no append, no literal switch.

6. **Be explicit about bookkeeping per outcome**:
   - **pure literal append fallback** → `buffer_modification_index = null`
   - **apply / override / cancel** → `buffer_modification_index` points at the modified existing vowel
   - **cancel** still uses the modified vowel index, even though it also appends a new literal trigger

7. **Add behavior tests that distinguish `S` from `F`/`J`**:
   - empty buffer + `S`/`s` → literal append, **no** `literal_index`
   - no-vowel pseudoword + `S`/`s` → literal append, **no** `literal_index`
   - unplaceable tone case + `S`/`s` → literal append, **no** `literal_index`
   - existing rising tone + `S`/`s` → cancel + literal append + **set** `literal_index`
   - existing non-rising tone + `S`/`s` → override, no literal append, no `literal_index`

3) **Rationale and trade-offs**

The biggest issue in the proposed taxonomy is that it risks importing **`F`/`J` behavior into `S`**. The spec’s formal literal-character rule names **`F`, `J`, `W`**, not `S`. That matters a lot:

- `F`/`J` become literal-mode triggers when not consumed.
- `S` is a legitimate Vietnamese consonant, so unused `S` must usually remain just a normal literal character inside ongoing Telex processing.

If you set `literal_index` for ordinary `S` fallback, you would incorrectly shut off Vietnamese processing for valid words that begin with or contain `s`.

So yes: **`S` should differ from `F`/`J` on ordinary fallback behavior**.

4) **Risks and guardrails**

- **Risk: conflating “not consumed” with “enter literal mode.”**  
  Guardrail: treat those as separate decisions. For `S`, most “not consumed” cases do **not** imply literal mode.

- **Risk: hiding normal fallback under `unreachable`.**  
  Guardrail: no-vowel and no-placeable cases must be explicit, because the spec defines them as normal literal fallback.

- **Risk: wrong condition ordering around cancel.**  
  Guardrail: decide from the scanned tone state. If matching rising tone exists, that is cancel, not a failed apply.

- **Risk: inconsistent `buffer_modification_index`.**  
  Guardrail: every action class should have one fixed rule:
  - literal append only → `null`
  - mutate existing span → modified index

5) **When to consider the advanced path**

Only revisit with a more shared design if you are about to implement **multiple tone branches (`F/R/X/J/Z`) in succession** and want to avoid five near-duplicate decision trees diverging over time. A good trigger is “we’ve implemented 3+ tone branches and are copying scan/state classification logic.”

6) **Optional advanced path**

Factor the tone branches around a shared internal classification of:
- scanned pseudoword range
- legal targetability
- existing tone state: none / matching / different

Then each trigger branch just maps that classification to its own behavior. But for just `S`, a direct branch implementation is still the simplest and best fit for the current style.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Literal input characters
8: 
9: - When any of the follow characters added to the word, change to literal input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal Vietnamese spelling. `Z` existing in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_
10: - Literal input remains until the new word.
11: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
12: - This rule is lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.
13: 
14: ### Diacritics
15: 
16: Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
17: Tones have a separate section.
18: 
19: #### Empty
20: 
21: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
22: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
23:   characters.
24: 
25: #### Circumflex
26: 
27: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
28: 
29: #### Breve
30: 
31: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
32: 
33: #### Horn
34: 
35: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
36: 
37: #### Stroke
38: 
39: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
40: 
41: #### Apply rules
42: 
43: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
44: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
45:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
46:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
47:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
48: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
49:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
50:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
51:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
52: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
53: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
54: 
55: #### Override rules
56: 
57: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
58:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
59:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
60: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
61: - Keep tone untouched.
62: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
63: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
64: 
65: #### Cancel rules
66: 
67: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
68:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
69: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
70:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
71:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
72:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
73:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
74: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
75: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
76:   remains until the new word.
77:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
78: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
79: 
80: #### Auto-fill rules
81: 
82: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
83:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
84: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
85:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
86:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
87:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
88: - The new input character is appended literally.
89: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
90: 
91: ### Tones
92: 
93: Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
94: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
95: These vowel could take any tone.
96: 
97: #### Level (ngang)
98: 
99: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
100:   corresponding variants.
101: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
102: 
103: #### Rising (sắc)
104: 
105: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
106: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
107:   -> `Ớ`.
108: 
109: #### Falling (huyền)
110: 
111: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
112: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
113:   -> `Ờ`.
114: 
115: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
116: 
117: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
118: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
119:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
120: 
121: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
122: 
123: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
124: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
125:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
126: 
127: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
128: 
129: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
130: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
131:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
132: 
133: #### Apply rules
134: 
135: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
136:   character literally.
137: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
138:   their lower cases.
139: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
140: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
141: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
142:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
143: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
144: 
145: #### Cancel rules
146: 
147: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
148:   character literally.
149: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
150:   their lower cases.
151: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
152: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
153: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
154:   the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
155: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
156:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
157: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
158: 
159: #### Reset rules
160: 
161: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
162:   character literally.
163: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
164: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
165: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
166:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
167: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
168:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
169: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
170:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
171: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
172: 
173: #### Override rules
174: 
175: - Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
176:   character literally.
177: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
178:   their lower cases.
179: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
180: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
181: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
182:   from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
183:   the word. The override tone is always successful.
184: 
185: #### Scan pseudo-word
186: 
187: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
188:   the tone in the correct position.
189: - The existing characters must not be empty.
190: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
191: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
192:   character to scan, then stop.
193: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
194:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
195: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
196:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
197:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
198: 
199: #### Tone position rules
200: 
201: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
202: 
203: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
204:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
205: - Exceptions:
206:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
207: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
208: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
209:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
210:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
211:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
212:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
213:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
214:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
215:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
216:     consonants, put on second vowel.
217:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
218: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
219: - Could not put tone for other cases.

input.goal: Extract the context relevant to implementing the S/s branch in State.add for the Vietnamese Telex input method, especially existing style constraints, branch taxonomy discussion, and any noted edge cases or expectations.

tool_uses.result:
### Goal: Implement 'S'/'s' branch in `State.add` for Vietnamese Telex

#### 1. Intent-Level Taxonomy (Proposed Approach)
Based on the discussion, the approach to handling the 'S'/'s' (Rising/Sắc tone) branch follows this logic:
- **Scan the pseudoword**: Identify the vowel group and surrounding consonants to determine tone placement.
- **Handle literal cases first**: Check if the engine is in literal mode, if a `literal_index` is already set, or if the buffer is empty.
- **Handle Tone Actions**:
    - **Apply**: If the pseudoword has vowels and currently has a 'Level' (ngang) tone, apply the rising tone using position rules.
    - **Override**: If the pseudoword has a different tone (falling, dipping-rising, etc.), reset it to level and apply the rising tone.
    - **Cancel**: If the pseudoword already has a rising tone, reset it to level and append the trigger character 's'/'S' literally, then switch to literal mode from that position.
- **Handle Out of Scope**: If no vowels are found or the word is a consonant-only exception (like `QU`), append literally.

#### 2. Specification Source of Truth (Telex Rules)
- **Trigger**: `S`, `s` puts the rising tone (sắc) on Vietnamese vowels (e.g., `As` -> `Á`).
- **Scan Pseudo-word**: Iterate backward from the last character. If a vowel is found, continue backward until the first consonant or start of buffer. If a consonant is found first, go backward to find the vowel, then the preceding consonant.
- **Tone Position Rules (Priority Order)**:
    1. If vowels contain `Ơ`, put tone on `Ơ`.
    2. If vowels contain `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư`, put on the first one found in this priority list.
    3. `GI`, `QU` exceptions: If the word starts with these, the `I` or `U` counts as part of the consonant; put tone on the next vowel.
    4. `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY`: 
        - If exactly these vowels: put on the first vowel.
        - If followed by more vowels or consonants: put on the second vowel.
    5. Otherwise: Put on the first vowel.
- **Cancellation**: If the triggered tone matches the existing tone, reset to level, append the trigger literally, and set `literal_index` to the current position.

#### 3. Existing Code Patterns and Style Constraints
Existing branches (like `A`, `D`, `W`) in `src/liblex.zig` establish the following conventions:
- **Literal Check**: 
  ```zig
  if (self.literal_index != null or self.mode == .literal or self.buffer_length == 0) {
      self.append_literal(c);
      self.buffer_modification_index = null;
  }
  ```
- **Modification Tracking**: `self.buffer_modification_index` must be updated to the index of the span that was changed (or set to `null` if only appending).
- **Span Construction**: Use `Span.init_diacritic_tone(base, diacritic, tone)`.
- **Literal Transition**: When a rule triggers a switch to literal input (like cancellation), set `self.literal_index = self.buffer_length`.

#### 4. Test Expectations and Edge Cases
Relevant test cases from `src/liblex.zig` for the rising tone include:
- **Single Vowels**: `a` + `s` -> `á`.
- **Complex Vowels**: 
    - `oa` -> `óa` (Exact OA, first vowel).
    - `oan` -> `oán` (Extended OA, second vowel).
    - `uy` -> `úy` (Exact UY, first vowel).
    - `uyn` -> `uýn` (Extended UY, second vowel).
- **Consonant Clusters**: 
    - `gia` -> `giá` (GI special, tone on `a`).
    - `qua` -> `quá` (QU special, tone on `a`).
- **Priority Vowels**:
    - `uơp` -> `uớp` (Ơ priority).
    - `tiên` -> `tiến` (Ê priority).
    - `ăê` -> `ăế` (Ê priority over Ă).
- **Multi-syllable/Suffix**: `vanhoa` + `s` -> `vanhóa` (Only the last pseudo-word `hoa` is considered).

progress.message:
1) **TL;DR**

Your taxonomy is close, but it needs one crucial split: **“append literally” is not the same as “switch to literal mode.”** For **`S`/`s`**, ordinary fallback should usually be **literal append only**; unlike **`F`/`J`/`W`**, it should **not** set `literal_index` just because the trigger was not consumed. The one clear exception is **tone cancel**, where the spec explicitly says to append the trigger literally **and** switch to literal from that new position.

2) **Recommended approach (simple path, M 1–3h including tests)**

1. **Keep the shared early passthrough gate**, but keep it narrow:
   - already in literal mode
   - `literal_index` already active
   - empty buffer  
   These should all short-circuit before scan.
   - Minor critique: **“overflow” is not really its own behavioral category** here; in this codebase it is already represented by `literal_index` having been set earlier.

2. **After the early gate, scan the pseudoword first**, as you proposed.  
   That is the right pivot for tone handling.

3. **Split the pre-mutation fallback outcomes more explicitly** than “literal/out-of-scope/no-placeable”:
   - **no vowels in pseudoword**
   - **has vowels, but no legal tone position exists**
   - optionally treat exact `QU`-style non-placeable cases as part of that second bucket  
   These are normal, user-visible fallbacks, not exceptional cases.

4. **Then classify by existing tone state, not by verb order.**
   The clean partition is:
   - **matching rising tone already present** → **cancel**
   - **different non-level tone present** → **override**
   - **all scanned vowels are level** → **apply**

   That is clearer than “apply, override, cancel” as a priority list, because those are not really competing actions; they are mutually exclusive states of the scanned pseudoword.

5. **For `S` specifically, keep literal-mode switching only on cancel.**
   - **Empty / no vowel / no placeable tone**: append `S`/`s` literally, **do not** enter literal mode.
   - **Cancel**: remove the rising tone, append `S`/`s` literally, **do** enter literal mode from the new character position.
   - **Apply / override**: mutate in place only; no append, no literal switch.

6. **Be explicit about bookkeeping per outcome**:
   - **pure literal append fallback** → `buffer_modification_index = null`
   - **apply / override / cancel** → `buffer_modification_index` points at the modified existing vowel
   - **cancel** still uses the modified vowel index, even though it also appends a new literal trigger

7. **Add behavior tests that distinguish `S` from `F`/`J`**:
   - empty buffer + `S`/`s` → literal append, **no** `literal_index`
   - no-vowel pseudoword + `S`/`s` → literal append, **no** `literal_index`
   - unplaceable tone case + `S`/`s` → literal append, **no** `literal_index`
   - existing rising tone + `S`/`s` → cancel + literal append + **set** `literal_index`
   - existing non-rising tone + `S`/`s` → override, no literal append, no `literal_index`

3) **Rationale and trade-offs**

The biggest issue in the proposed taxonomy is that it risks importing **`F`/`J` behavior into `S`**. The spec’s formal literal-character rule names **`F`, `J`, `W`**, not `S`. That matters a lot:

- `F`/`J` become literal-mode triggers when not consumed.
- `S` is a legitimate Vietnamese consonant, so unused `S` must usually remain just a normal literal character inside ongoing Telex processing.

If you set `literal_index` for ordinary `S` fallback, you would incorrectly shut off Vietnamese processing for valid words that begin with or contain `s`.

So yes: **`S` should differ from `F`/`J` on ordinary fallback behavior**.

4) **Risks and guardrails**

- **Risk: conflating “not consumed” with “enter literal mode.”**  
  Guardrail: treat those as separate decisions. For `S`, most “not consumed” cases do **not** imply literal mode.

- **Risk: hiding normal fallback under `unreachable`.**  
  Guardrail: no-vowel and no-placeable cases must be explicit, because the spec defines them as normal literal fallback.

- **Risk: wrong condition ordering around cancel.**  
  Guardrail: decide from the scanned tone state. If matching rising tone exists, that is cancel, not a failed apply.

- **Risk: inconsistent `buffer_modification_index`.**  
  Guardrail: every action class should have one fixed rule:
  - literal append only → `null`
  - mutate existing span → modified index

5) **When to consider the advanced path**

Only revisit with a more shared design if you are about to implement **multiple tone branches (`F/R/X/J/Z`) in succession** and want to avoid five near-duplicate decision trees diverging over time. A good trigger is “we’ve implemented 3+ tone branches and are copying scan/state classification logic.”

6) **Optional advanced path**

Factor the tone branches around a shared internal classification of:
- scanned pseudoword range
- legal targetability
- existing tone state: none / matching / different

Then each trigger branch just maps that classification to its own behavior. But for just `S`, a direct branch implementation is still the simplest and best fit for the current style.

   200	
   201	The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
   202

content.text:
I agree with the broad shape of your idea, but I would adjust the taxonomy around one distinction:

**“append the `S`/`s` literally” is not the same as “switch to literal input.”**

That distinction matters because the spec’s literal-mode trigger list is only `F`, `J`, `W`, not `S` ([literal input rule](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L7-L12)). `S` is a normal Vietnamese consonant, so failed tone handling should usually append `s` and keep Telex processing active.

## Suggested intent-level decision tree for `S` / `s`

### 1. Early passthrough / no-processing cases

Before scanning pseudoword:

- If already in `.literal` mode: append literal, no modification.
- If `literal_index` is already active: append literal, no modification.
- If buffer is empty: append `S`/`s` literally, no modification, **do not set `literal_index`**.

This follows the existing branch style in `State.add`: early conditions first, then rule-specific handling, and every path sets `buffer_modification_index` ([existing pattern](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L276-L315)).

Important difference from `F`/`J`/`W`: existing tests currently expect `F`, `J`, `W` on empty buffer to set `literal_index` ([test](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L1617-L1640)). I would not apply that behavior to `S`.

### 2. Scan pseudoword

After the early gate, scan the existing buffer according to the spec’s pseudoword rule:

- scan backward from the last existing character;
- form one of the specified shapes:
  - `<consonants>`
  - `<vowels><consonants>`
  - `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`
  - `<vowels>`
  - `<consonant><vowels>`

Reference: [scan pseudoword spec](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L187-L197).

The existing `Pseudoword` struct already suggests the intended data needed for this: word start/end and vowel start/end ([struct](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L210-L222)).

### 3. Determine whether tone placement is possible

Before deciding apply/cancel/override, determine whether the pseudoword has a valid tone target under the tone-position rules.

Normal “no target” cases:

- pseudoword has no vowel;
- exact `QU` exception;
- tone-position rules cannot choose a target.

Reference: [apply fallback](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L135-L143), [tone-position rules](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L199-L219).

For `S`/`s`, these cases should mean:

- append `S`/`s` literally;
- `buffer_modification_index = null`;
- **do not set `literal_index`**, except for the generic buffer-overflow behavior if the append crosses the effective buffer boundary.

This is where `S` should diverge from the existing `F`/`J` tests that switch to literal on no-placeable-tone cases ([F/J no-placeable tests](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L1692-L1781)).

### 4. Classify by existing tone state

Once there is a placeable pseudoword, classify the scanned vowels by tone state.

I would not order this as “apply, override, cancel.” Those are better understood as mutually exclusive states:

#### A. All scanned vowels are level

Intent: **apply rising tone**.

- Use tone-position rules to find target vowel.
- Replace that vowel with same base + same diacritic + `.rising`.
- Do not append the trigger.
- `buffer_length` unchanged.
- `literal_index` unchanged/null.
- `buffer_modification_index = target vowel index`.

This is exactly the current rising-tone test surface: valid level-toned pseudowords receive `.rising` at the expected index, with `literal_index == null` ([rising tests](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L1783-L1887)).

#### B. A scanned vowel already has `.rising`

Intent: **cancel rising tone**.

Spec condition: matching triggered tone exists; reset it to level and append the trigger literally ([cancel rule](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L145-L157)).

Outcome:

- reset the existing `.rising` vowel to `.level`;
- preserve base and diacritic;
- append `S`/`s` literally;
- switch to literal from the new character position;
- `buffer_modification_index = modified vowel index`.

This is the main case where `S`/`s` literal append should also set `literal_index`.

#### C. A scanned vowel has a non-level tone different from `.rising`

Intent: **override tone**.

Spec condition: existing non-level tone differs from the triggered tone; reset old tone and apply new tone using tone-position rules. Override is always successful ([override rule](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L173-L183)).

Outcome:

- remove the old non-level tone;
- apply `.rising` to the target chosen by tone-position rules;
- do not append `S`/`s`;
- do not set `literal_index`;
- `buffer_modification_index = final modified vowel index`.

If the old tone and new tone are on different vowels after repositioning, the implementation later needs to be careful about modification index semantics. Intent-wise, the earliest affected index may be the safer mental model because replacement/backspace calculation cares about the first changed span.

### 5. Unreachable only after explicit normal fallbacks

`unreachable` should only cover logically impossible combinations after the above categories are exhaustive.

Do not use `unreachable` for:

- no vowel;
- exact `QU`;
- no valid tone target;
- already literal mode;
- empty buffer;
- `S` used as a normal consonant.

Those are normal user-input states, not internal impossibilities.

## Case count summary

For `S`/`s`, I would think in these behavioral cases:

1. Existing literal mode / active `literal_index`: literal append, no mutation.
2. Empty buffer: literal append, no literal-mode switch.
3. No vowel in scanned pseudoword: literal append, no literal-mode switch.
4. Vowels exist but no legal tone target: literal append, no literal-mode switch.
5. All scanned vowels level: apply `.rising`.
6. Matching `.rising` already present: cancel, append literal `S`/`s`, switch to literal from appended position.
7. Different non-level tone present: override to `.rising`.
8. Anything else: unreachable.

## Oracle critique incorporated

Oracle agreed with the overall scan/classify approach, but highlighted the key correction: **separate literal append from literal-mode switching**.

Its main point: `S` should not inherit `F`/`J` fallback behavior. `F` and `J` are in the spec’s non-Vietnamese literal-mode trigger list; `S` is not. So ordinary fallback for `S` should keep Telex active. Only tone cancellation explicitly appends the trigger and switches to literal mode.

My idea:

- I will break down the handling to the flow:
  - Scan pseudoword: a helper function of State, named `pseudo_word` to scan the pseudoword for any tone applying, reusable.
  - Apply tone: a helper function of State, named `apply_tone(word: Pseudoword, tone: Tone)`, reusable for any tone.
  - The condition to call these functions belong to each branch.

Side question:

- How can we skip the rest of the switch branch inside an `if` statement, e.g. when append literal, then we don't want to scan pseudoword.

Continue the discussion.

import urllib.request, re
text=urllib.request.urlopen('https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/', timeout=20).read().decode('utf-8','ignore')
for anchor in ['#Blocks','id="Blocks"','id="switch"','id="Labeled-switch"','id="break"']:
    i=text.find(anchor)
    print(anchor, i)
# dump around headings with plain html stripped rough
for key in ['id="Blocks"', 'id="switch"', 'id="Labeled-switch"']:
    i=text.find(key)
    chunk=text[i:i+5000]
    chunk=re.sub('<[^>]+>','',chunk)
    chunk=re.sub('"','"',chunk)
    chunk=re.sub('&','&',chunk)
    chunk=re.sub('<','<',chunk)
    chunk=re.sub('>','>',chunk)
    print('\n---',key,'---')
    print(chunk[:3000])
PY

content.thinking:
**Considering naming conventions**

I think it’s better to avoid using “return” here since `State.add` has an end invariant. Instead, I might go with a branch-local labeled block or nested if-else statements. I’m also pondering the naming for “pseudoword.” The user suggests `pseudo_word` for consistency with the existing type `Pseudoword`, but I wonder if just `pseudoword` could be better, considering language norms in Zig. It seems like using `pseudoword` might be less common, but it could fit well.

content.text:
- pseudo_word -> yes, use `pseudoword`.
- apply_tone -> make it simple, the pseudoword must be ready for applying tone, and always applied successfully: vowels are level, cause `unreachable` when could not apply tone. The checking must belong to the branch handling.

Agreed. With those constraints, the helper boundary becomes cleaner.

## Naming

Use:

- `pseudoword`
- `apply_tone`

`pseudoword` matches the existing `Pseudoword` type and treats the term as one domain word.

## Revised helper responsibilities

### `State.pseudoword`

Intent:

- Scan the current effective buffer.
- Return the pseudoword/vowel range information.
- Do not mutate.
- Do not append.
- Do not classify tone state beyond what is structurally needed to scan.
- Do not know which trigger caused the scan.

It answers only:

> “What is the current pseudoword range relevant to tone rules?”

### `State.apply_tone(word: Pseudoword, tone: Tone)`

Given your clarification, this helper should be narrower and stricter:

Preconditions:

- `word` has already been scanned.
- `word` is valid for tone placement.
- `word` has at least one vowel.
- all relevant vowels are `.level`.
- branch logic has already ruled out cancel/override/fallback.
- tone-position rules must be able to choose a target.

Intent:

- Use tone-position rules.
- Mutate exactly one vowel to `tone`.
- Preserve base, case, and diacritic.
- Set `buffer_modification_index` to the changed vowel.
- Do not append the trigger.
- Do not set `literal_index`.
- If no target can be selected, that is an internal bug and should be `unreachable`.

That makes `apply_tone` a “trusted operation,” not a classifier.

## Consequence for `S` / `s` branch

The `S` branch must do all validity checks before calling `apply_tone`.

Intent flow:

1. Early passthrough:
   - literal mode;
   - active `literal_index`;
   - empty buffer.

2. Scan:
   - `const word = self.pseudoword();`

3. Branch-owned fallback checks:
   - no vowels;
   - exact `QU`;
   - any other structurally unplaceable tone case.

   For `S`, these append literal `S`/`s` and do not set `literal_index`.

4. Branch-owned tone-state classification:
   - all vowels level → safe to call `apply_tone(word, .rising)`;
   - matching `.rising` exists → cancel;
   - different non-level tone exists → override;
   - inconsistent multiple-tone state, if considered impossible by engine invariants → `unreachable`.

5. `apply_tone` is called only in the all-level apply path, and possibly in the override path **after** the branch has reset the old tone to level.

## Override with this helper contract

For override, the branch should not call `apply_tone` until it has made the word compatible with `apply_tone`’s preconditions.

Intent:

1. detect existing non-level tone different from `.rising`;
2. reset that old tone to `.level`;
3. now the word should be level-toned;
4. call `apply_tone(word, .rising)`.

One caveat: if the old tone vowel and the new tone target are different, two existing spans change. That affects `buffer_modification_index`.

Given the project rule that every `State.add` action must decide `buffer_modification_index`, this needs an explicit policy before implementation:

- either `apply_tone` always sets it to the new tone target, and override branch corrects it to the earliest changed index if needed;
- or override is not allowed to delegate bookkeeping completely to `apply_tone`.

I would choose:

> `apply_tone` sets `buffer_modification_index` for simple apply; override branch may adjust it if it changed another earlier vowel first.

That keeps `apply_tone` simple while preserving correct branch-level responsibility.

## Cancel with this helper contract

Cancel does not use `apply_tone`.

Intent:

- find the vowel with matching `.rising`;
- reset that vowel to `.level`;
- set `buffer_modification_index` to that vowel;
- set `literal_index` to current `buffer_length`;
- append literal `S`/`s`.

This mirrors the diacritic cancel pattern: mutate existing span, then append literal trigger, then enter literal input from the appended position.

## Unreachable placement

With your stricter `apply_tone`, `unreachable` belongs in two places:

### Inside `apply_tone`

For impossible tone-position failure after the branch promised the word is apply-ready.

Example intent:

> “If no tone target is found here, the caller violated the precondition.”

### Inside branch classification

For impossible tone-state combinations, if the engine invariant is one tone per pseudoword.

But normal user input must not be unreachable:

- no vowel;
- exact `QU`;
- no placeable tone;
- existing matching tone;
- existing different tone;
- literal mode;
- empty buffer.

Those are normal branches.
